summary: I began writing plays with the Carolina Playmakers in the 1920s and 1930s. This group was where I first expressed my artistic voice, especially during the Depression era. It was a formative experience that shaped my perspective on poverty and social issues in North Carolina.

The Great Depression of the 1930s was a familiar condition to me, as poverty was something I knew growing up in Harnett County. While it didn't shock me, it deepened my understanding of economic hardship and influenced much of my writing about tenant farmers and disenfranchised people. The Depression raised questions about democracy and justice that I pondered deeply during those years.

World War I was a significant event that shaped my early worldview. I regarded it as a foolish and insane war, though I understood the reasons behind it. The war's aftermath and the social conditions it left behind were part of the backdrop of my youth and artistic reflections.

The disappointment in leaders like Warren G. Harding during the post-World War I era affected me as much as it did others. His presidency was part of the political context that influenced my thinking about leadership and the failures of the time. These experiences fed into my broader reflections on society and governance.

Calvin Coolidge's presidency, like Harding's, was a period of political disappointment for me. The era's leadership failures contributed to the social and economic problems that I witnessed and wrote about. These presidents were part of the historical fabric that shaped my understanding of American democracy.

Woodrow Wilson's dream of a united world through the League of Nations was a great hope that ultimately failed. This failure was a source of disillusionment for me and many others, influencing my perspective on international politics and peace efforts after World War I. Wilson's vision and its collapse were part of the historical context I lived through.

The League of Nations represented Woodrow Wilson's hope for global unity after World War I, but its failure was deeply disappointing. This failure shaped my understanding of international relations and the limits of idealism in politics. It was part of the broader social and political environment of my youth.

I grew up in Harnett County, North Carolina, where poverty was a constant reality. The effects of the Civil War were still visible during my childhood, and life there was very rural and underdeveloped. This environment deeply influenced my writing and my understanding of social and economic hardship.

The American Civil War's impact was still felt in eastern North Carolina when I was a boy. Many old soldiers shared their stories, and the war's shadow loomed over the region's poverty and social conditions. I came to see the war as a great, foolish tragedy that shaped much of the history I grew up with.

John Steinbeck was a contemporary writer who captured the spirit of the Depression in his work. His novel, The Grapes of Wrath, was inspired by the Dust Bowl and the economic hardships of the 1930s. While his work was influenced by the Depression, my own writing was shaped more by the poverty I knew from childhood.

The Grapes of Wrath by John Steinbeck was a powerful novel born out of the Depression and Dust Bowl hardships. It reflected the struggles of people during that era, and I recognized its importance as a cultural response to those times. Though it didn't change my personal experience, it was a significant work of the period.

The Second World War brought a new sense of fear and unity to the country. It changed the social climate by introducing rationing and controls, which made people cooperate in ways they hadn't before. This war shifted the national mood and delayed some of the social and economic questions raised by the Depression.

After the Depression, we were told by officials in Washington that the Federal Reserve System and other safeguards would prevent another economic collapse. These assurances gave people hope that bank failures and depressions were things of the past. However, later events showed that these protections were not foolproof, leaving me confused about the remedies offered.

I was suspicious of the New Deal when it began because I believed in hard work and responsibility. I remember seeing men with shovels doing work that a steam shovel could easily do, and I thought it was charity rather than a real solution. However, I did appreciate programs like the CCC, where young men did meaningful work planting sea oats and protecting the coast.

While working on a drama in Manteo, North Carolina, I observed the Civilian Conservation Corps boys planting sea oats along the dunes. This work impressed me as a positive aspect of the New Deal, showing how government programs could provide meaningful employment. Manteo was a place where I saw the practical side of these efforts firsthand.

The Civilian Conservation Corps was a government program during the New Deal that I saw in action in places like Manteo. The young men in the CCC did valuable work planting sea oats and protecting the environment. I thought this was a worthwhile effort, unlike some other New Deal projects that seemed inefficient.

The Works Progress Administration was another New Deal agency that provided jobs during the Depression. I saw WPA workers involved in conservation efforts like those of the CCC, which I respected. These programs were part of the government's attempt to address unemployment, though I had mixed feelings about their overall effectiveness.

I mentioned Exxon oil people as an example of entities that think only in terms of profit, contributing to the conflicts in our democracy. This attitude, focused solely on gain without responsibility, is part of the problem I see in society. It contrasts with the balance I believe is necessary between liberty and responsibility.

Judge Braxton Craven spoke recently to law students about the dangers of losing freedoms in exchange for jobs and safety. His words resonated with me, as I see the tension between liberty and security growing in our society. Craven warned that people might ultimately give up constitutional rights to avoid hardship and violence.

I spent time in Russia and was struck by the contrast in public safety compared to the U.S. I could walk freely at night in cities like Leningrad and Moscow without fear, which I cannot do in Washington or New York. This experience made me reflect on the social conditions and fears present in America today.

While in Leningrad, Russia, I felt safe walking the streets at any hour, a freedom I don't have in American cities. This safety was striking and made me think about the social order and fears in the United States. Leningrad showed me a different side of urban life during my travels.

In Moscow, like in Leningrad, I could walk freely at night without fear for my life. This was a stark contrast to my experiences in Washington and New York. My time in Moscow gave me a perspective on how fear shapes public behavior and social conditions in different countries.

In Washington, D.C., I do not feel safe walking at night, unlike my experience in Russian cities. The fear for personal safety is very strong here, reflecting broader social issues. Washington is also where government officials assured us that another Depression could not happen, though recent events have challenged that belief.

New York City is another place where I do not dare walk at night due to safety concerns. This fear contrasts sharply with my experiences in Russian cities. New York represents the challenges of urban life and social disorder that I observe in America today.

Jonathan Daniels was a good friend of mine who served as Press Secretary for President Roosevelt and later edited the News & Observer. He was optimistic about America's ability to 'muddle along' through difficult times. His perspective provided a counterpoint to my own concerns about leadership and social conditions.

Franklin D. Roosevelt was president during the Depression and the New Deal era, a time when I was both hopeful and suspicious of government efforts. His administration launched programs like the CCC and WPA, which I saw in action. Roosevelt's leadership shaped much of the social and political landscape I lived through.

The News & Observer was the newspaper my friend Jonathan Daniels edited after serving as Press Secretary for Roosevelt. It was a significant voice in the region and reflected many of the political and social issues of the time. Through it, I stayed connected to broader discussions about the country's future.
